Mercedes‑AMG GT / GT S / GT C Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑AMG GT / GT S / GT C engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M178 engine typically costs £5,000–£10,000 supply only depending on variant, with the GT C sitting at £7,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £1,100–£1,800. Main dealer new engines can exceed £25,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑AMG GT / GT S / GT C engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M178 is rated ★★★½☆ — it's considered a robust and remarkably durable engine. Owners have logged tens of thousands of miles with tuned engines producing over 700 bhp without significant issues. However, known issues include wastegate actuator/boost problems, stuck injectors, dynamic engine mount failures, and transmission oil leaks.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑AMG GT / GT S / GT C?

acceptedAnswer.text: Wastegate actuator/boost issues, dynamic engine and transmission mount failures (particularly GT C/GT S), stuck injectors, faulty exhaust flaps, and transmission oil leaks. The engine is generally robust with proper maintenance — 200,000–300,000 km examples are not uncommon.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑AMG GT engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept AMG GT worth £25,000–£60,000, a £7,500 recon is a manageable 12–30% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a well‑maintained example. Given the AMG GT's status and strong retained values, replacement is usually viable for vehicles in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑AMG GT engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The AMG GT / GT S / GT C uses the M178 4.0‑litre twin‑turbo V8. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M178's wastegate actuator, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the wastegate actuator and boost control issues are repairable, typically costing £500–£1,500. If caught early, it does not require engine replacement. At the first sign of power loss or whistling, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The AMG GT / GT S / GT C THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Entry‑level M178 — robust and tunable; wastegate and cooling system are the main watchpoints.
Mid‑range M178 — the sweet spot; dynamic engine mounts can fail.
Top‑spec M178 — 550 bhp, rear‑wheel steering; stuck injectors and transmission leaks are the main watchpoints.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes‑AMG GT / GT S / GT C Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs, warranty claims; dealer costs can exceed £25,000

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an AMG GT engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The M178's hot‑vee layout requires significant disassembly.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes‑AMG GT / GT S / GT C — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Specialist repair; catch it before turbo damage
Replace with updated parts; common on GT C/GT S
Replace failed injectors; catch it before cat damage
Replace exhaust flap actuators; common issue
Some model years have recalls; check before buying
Rare but possible; regular oil changes prevent

buyingChecks.title: Buying This AMG GT / GT S / GT C? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with documented oil changes (every 5,000–7,000 miles)
* Evidence of dynamic mount replacement on GT C/GT S
* Good service history — the M178 is robust but demands care
avoidIf: * Loss of power or boost issues with no diagnosis
* Vibration or clunking — potential dynamic mount failure
* Rough idle or misfire codes — injector issues
* Transmission oil leaks — some model years have recalls
* Poor service history — the AMG GT is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes‑AMG GT is a masterpiece — a hand‑built V8 sports car that delivers phenomenal performance. The M178 engine is robust and remarkably tunable, with owners reporting over 700 bhp on stock internals without issues. However, it has known weaknesses: wastegate actuators, dynamic engine mounts, stuck injectors, and transmission leaks. Buy on service history and proof of preventative maintenance. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12‑month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑AMG GT / GT S / GT C (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain issues are rare but possible)
* Service History — Including which engine variant (GT, GT S, or GT C) — see the Era Map above
* Boost Health — Test acceleration; any loss of power or whistling? Wastegate actuator risk
* Vibrations — Any vibration through the cabin? Dynamic engine/transmission mount failure indicator
* Idle Quality — Any rough idle or misfire? Stuck injector risk
* Exhaust Flaps — Do the exhaust flaps work correctly? Faulty actuators are common
* Transmission Leaks — Any oil leaks from the transmission case? Some model years have recalls
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved boost, injector, or mount issues?
